perturbation.original_text: Charity Tunes shall make its music catalogue available to the purchasers in MP3 file format to the fullest extent that such format is available to Charity Tunes pursuant to any agreements in effect between Charity Tunes and its wholesalers on the date of this Agreement. Accordingly, Charity Tunes shall use its best efforts to update its music catalogue with available MP3 files by June 30, 2009.
perturbation.changed_text: Charity Tunes shall endeavor to make its music catalogue available to the purchasers in MP3 file format to the extent deemed reasonably practical by Charity Tunes, considering its agreements with wholesalers. Charity Tunes will aim to update its music catalogue with MP3 files by June 30, 2009.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor to make available' and 'to the extent deemed reasonably practical' introduce subjectivity and make the obligation less enforceable. Replacing 'shall use its best efforts' with 'will aim to' further dilutes the commitment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Competition Act (R.S.C., 1985, c. C-34) - Misleading Advertising
perturbation.law_citation: Competition Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-34, s. 52
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows Charity Tunes to avoid providing the promised MP3 downloads if they deem it 'not reasonably practical', potentially misleading consumers who were promised specific access to music downloads, which violates Section 52 of the Competition Act (Canada) regarding misleading advertising and promotional offers.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Competition Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu Table of Contents Related Information Related Provisions Amendments Not In Force Amendments * Amendment Citation Amendment date 2023, c. 31 2024-12-15 2024, c. 15 2024-12-15 2024, c. 15 2024-06-20 2023, c. 31 2023-12-15 2022, c. 10 2023-06-23 2022, c. 10 2022-06-23 2020, c. 1 2020-07-01 2019, c. 25 2019-12-18 2014, c. 20, s. 366 2019-06-17 2014, c. 20, s. 366(1) 2019-06-17 * List of amendments since 2019-01-01 (limited to last 10 amendments) [more details] Regulations made under this Act Anti-Competitive Acts of Persons Operating a Domestic Service, Regulations Respecting Â (SOR/2000-324) Notifiable Transactions Regulations Â (SOR/87-348) Repealed regulations made under this Act Restrictive Trade Practices Commission Rules [Repealed] Â (C.R.C., c. 416) ï»¿ Date modified: 2025-06-12 ï»¿
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity, weakening the original obligation. The original text stated Charity Tunes 'shall use its best efforts', while the modified text uses 'endeavor to make available' and 'to the extent deemed reasonably practical'. This creates ambiguity and makes the obligation less enforceable, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation states this could mislead consumers and violate Section 52 of the Competition Act regarding misleading advertising. The first snippet of the Competition Act does not give the full text, so it is hard to see exactly how it might violate the law, but the LLM Law Explanation suggests that the change to the contract would mislead consumers, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: The custom interactive landing page shall be designed, operated and maintained at the expense of Sponsor, and shall be subject to the mutual approval by Charity Tunes and Sponsor, which approval shall not be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.changed_text: The custom interactive landing page shall be designed, operated and maintained at the expense of Sponsor, and shall be subject to the approval by both Charity Tunes and Sponsor, which approval may be withheld.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'which approval shall not be unreasonably withheld' gives both parties the ability to withhold approval for any reason, or no reason at all. This creates an ambiguous obligation as the provision is now open to arbitrary application. The original requirement would have created an implied expectation of good faith, which is now lost.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing either party to arbitrarily withhold approval, the modified text potentially violates the implied duty of good faith in contractual performance as established in *Bhasin v Hrynew*. This duty requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their contractual obligations. The modified text now allows for a party to act dishonestly by withholding approval without valid reason, contradicting the principles of good faith under Canadian Contract Law.

Boehner, McConnell Outline Priorities for 114th Congress In response to the Nov. 5 Congressional elections, which saw Republicans achieve the requisite number of electoral victories to secure a majority in the U.S. Senate, while retaining their majority in the House of Representatives, House Speaker Boehner and presumptive Majority Leader McConnell (R-KY) penned a joint op-ed in the Wall Street Journal outlining GOP priorities for the 114th Congress. Under a general theme of job creation and restoring trust in the legislative process, Boehner and McConnell pledged to pursue bipartisan legislative initiatives they claim had previously been stalled in the Democratic-controlled Senate in the 113th Congress. According to the op-ed, "[t]hese bills include measures authorizing the construction of the Keystone XL pipeline, which will mean lower energy costs for families and more jobs for American workers
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'not unreasonably withheld' clause, granting either party the ability to arbitrarily withhold approval. The LLM-generated Law Explanation references *Bhasin v Hrynew* and the implied duty of good faith, suggesting this change could violate that duty. While the provided snippets don't directly address this specific contract law principle, the Perturbation Explanation explicitly states that the original text created an 'implied expectation of good faith', which is now lost. Because the Ambiguities perturbation type is defined as creating a contradiction when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, and the change here allows for arbitrary application, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Sponsor agrees that it will not increase the list price to its customers of Designated Sponsor Products that form part of this promotion as compared to its otherwise identical products that do not form part of the promotion.
perturbation.changed_text: Sponsor agrees that it will endeavor not to significantly increase the list price to its customers of Designated Sponsor Products that form part of this promotion as compared to its otherwise identical products that do not form part of the promotion.
perturbation.explanation: The inclusion of 'endeavor not to significantly increase' introduces ambiguity. 'Endeavor' suggests an attempt rather than a guarantee, and 'significantly increase' is subjective and lacks a clear definition. This weakens the protection against price gouging during the promotion.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Competition Act (R.S.C., 1985, c. C-34) - Deceptive Marketing Practices
perturbation.law_citation: Competition Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-34, s. 74.05
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing for a potential increase in price that is deemed not 'significant', the modified text could result in deceptive marketing practices, as it undermines the intended benefit of the promotion. Consumers might be misled into believing they are receiving a promotion when the price has been subtly increased, conflicting with Section 74.05 of the Competition Act (Canada), which deals with deceptive marketing and promotional offers.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing a firm commitment ('will not increase') with a weaker, less definite one ('will endeavor not to significantly increase'). This weakens the original protection against price increases, creating an ambiguous legal obligation. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that 'endeavor' suggests an attempt rather than a guarantee and that 'significantly increase' is subjective. This creates ambiguity that conflicts with the spirit of consumer protection laws regarding marketing and promotions, where clarity and certainty are expected, as suggested by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 'Deceptive marketing practices', which supports the idea that ambiguous pricing could be seen as misleading.
